7 min read
Just now

--

Post 1: AI Has Already Changed How Designers Work
If you’ve ever opened any design software in the past year, you’ve probably noticed AI features that pop up everywhere such as background removal, layout suggestions, text-to-image generation and many more. Something that used to feel experimental is now just a normal part of the workflow. There are tools like Adobe Sensei, Canva’s Magic Design and DreamStudio that designers use to speed up the early stages of a project, generate quick concept drafts and knock out any repetitive tasks like resizing components for different platforms (Gyang, Jonah, & Bonaventure, 2025).
A 2025 study that surveyed 60 graphic communication designers found that 73% believe AI integration has had a positive impact on the profession (Gyang et al., 2025). This stat suggests most designers on the ground don’t see AI as a threat to avoid but instead as a tool actively reshaping how they work day to day.
The interesting part about this isn’t just that AI can produce visuals because according to Coursera (2026), AI isn’t eliminating any design roles but is actually shifting what’s expected of designers because the mechanical, repetitive side of design work is increasingly automated. This means the value of a designer is moving toward strategy and about knowing how to use AI tools, apply brand judgment and make decisions AI can’t make on its own. That same 2025 study also found 61% of designers agree the role of traditional graphic designers will shrink as AI integration grows (Gyang et al., 2025) but the researchers mention that it doesn’t mean designers will just disappear. It means designers spend less time doing the work by hand and more time reviewing and directing what the AI puts out.
Li et al. (2024) conducted a large review covering 33 different studies on AI in graphic design and found four main ways it’s being used. It automates and generates layouts, helps with image editing, supports artistic and creative work and can even predict how people emotionally respond to visuals. So, it’s not just one tool doing one job but rather it’s showing up at almost every stage of the design process, from the very first rough sketch all the way to testing how people actually respond to the finished product.
This matches what design schools are saying as well which is that AI works best as a creative partner rather than something that replaces designers fully (LCCA, 2024). The thing that is still unclear across all this research is exactly where the line lies between AI genuinely saving time for designers and it quietly taking over decisions that used to require real judgment and that’s a problem the rest of the posts digs into.
Post 2: AI Is Quietly Rebuilding the Print Side Too
Graphic communication isn’t just about what happens on a screen but a huge part of the field is what happens after a file leaves a designer’s hands and heads into actual production, which is where AI’s impact is bigger than most people realize even though it doesn’t get talked about nearly as much.
Modern print operations deal with a ton of moving parts all at once, and AI powered systems are now reading all of that in real time, catching bottlenecks before they even happen and rerouting jobs automatically whenever something changes (Print3 Magazine, 2026). Tasks that used to require a technician manually checking a file for prepress errors or things like missing fonts, wrong image resolution or color inconsistencies, can now get caught and flagged automatically before the job even reaches the press, which saves a lot of time that used to just get eaten up by manual checks (Print3 Magazine, 2026).
AI is also being used for quality control since camera systems powered by computer vision are able to monitor print jobs as they run and catch things like color variation, registration errors and ink density issues the moment they happen instead of after an entire run has already been wasted (Print3 Magazine, 2026). On top of that, predictive maintenance tools track sensor data from presses and other equipment so problems like unusual vibration or overheating get caught early, which also means maintenance can be scheduled ahead of time instead of dealing with a sudden breakdown in the middle of a job (Print3 Magazine, 2026).
What’s interesting about this is it actually lines up with what the Li et al. (2024) review found on the design side too, since one of their four paradigms was specifically about AI automating and generating layouts more efficiently, so the pattern ends up being pretty similar whether you’re talking about a designer working in Photoshop or a press operator running a job on the floor. In both cases AI is mainly being used to catch problems early and cut down on wasted time and material rather than actually replacing the person doing the job.
Print businesses are also using AI for customized packaging, direct mail campaigns and event materials that now get tailored to each customer automatically based on their own data (Print3 Magazine, 2026). This is basically the same personalization trend that’s already happening in digital design, just showing up on the print side now instead.
None of this means the press floor is basically running itself but it just means fewer people are stuck doing the repetitive manual checks and more people are watching over the systems and stepping in when something actually needs a human call, which is honestly the same shift that’s happening on the design side too.
Post 3: The Pushback and Where This Is Actually Headed
Everything covered so far makes AI in graphic communication sound like a pretty beneficial upgrade but it wouldn’t be fair to only explain that side of the story because there’s also a decent amount of pushback and legitimate concern coming from inside the industry itself and not just from outside critics.
One of the biggest issues people bring up is authorship and originality. AI models get trained on huge datasets built from existing work and a lot of the time the people who actually made that original work never agreed to having it used that way. That’s a big reason why AI outputs end up feeling like they’re just borrowing from other people’s work instead of being something truly original (Li et al., 2024). This same source also explains that AI systems can end up repeating whatever biases are already plugged into their training data which sometimes leads to visuals that rely on stereotypes or leave certain groups out, especially around things like race, gender or culture.
A 2025 study that surveyed 60 graphic communication designers in Nigeria backs this up with actual numbers, since 63% of them agreed that integrating AI raises real ethical issues in the job and that’s coming directly from people working in the field rather than outside commentators (Gyang et al., 2025). That same study also raises some concern that barely gets mentioned anywhere else which is cultural homogenization. Since most generative AI models are trained mostly on Western datasets, a lot of unique cultural design styles could end up getting pushed out or just overlooked and the researchers actually bring this up specifically when talking about African design (Gyang et al., 2025). This serves as a reminder that AI doesn’t affect every culture the same way.
There’s also the worry that designers who lean on AI too much could lose some of their own hands-on skills over time, like typography or colour theory, just from lack of practice (Li et al., 2024). On top of that, 61% of designers in the same Nigerian study think the traditional designer role will shrink as AI usage grows even though most of them don’t think AI should be avoided.
One thing to note is that not every company is even in favour of the implementation of AI. For example, Procreate is one of the most popular design apps out there and they have publicly refused to add AI features at all, saying it goes against what they believe design should be about (Johnson, 2025).
Predictions say AI is going to keep getting built more directly into everyday design and production tools (Johnson, 2025) and the Nigerian study says the real fix isn’t avoiding AI but it’s to teach designers to use it critically instead of blindly (Gyang et al., 2025). AI isn’t just some passing trend and it’s not replacing designers either but it’s just becoming a normal part of the job. The people who do well going forward will be the ones who know how to use it instead of just letting it decide everything for them.
References
Coursera Staff. (2026, May 27). Will AI replace graphic designers? Coursera. https://www.coursera.org/articles/will-ai-replace-graphic-designers
Gyang, S. S., Jonah, B., & Bonaventure, Z. (2025). The integration of artificial intelligence into graphic communication design profession: Opportunities and challenges. Path of Science, 11(10), 2030–2038. https://doi.org/10.22178/pos.123-25
Johnson, J. (2025, March 18). The future of AI in graphic design: What’s happening now and what’s next? Rocketspark. https://creativelaunchpad.rocketspark.com/blog/post/142840/the-future-of-ai-in-graphic-design-whats-happening-now-and-whats-next/
LCCA. (2024, October 18). The role of AI in graphic design: Opportunities and challenges. https://www.lcca.ac.uk/blog/graphic-design/the-role-of-ai-in-graphic-design-opportunities-and-challenges/
Li, H., Xue, T., Zhang, A., Luo, X., Kong, L., & Huang, G. (2024). The application and impact of artificial intelligence technology in graphic design: A critical interpretive synthesis. Heliyon, 10(21), e40037. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2024.e40037
Print3 Magazine. (2026, June 30). How AI is transforming the printing industry in 2026: From automation to smarter production. https://www.print3.in/how-ai-is-transforming-the-printing-industry-in-2026-from-automation-to-smarter-production/
